Campbell
Campbell is a researcher who, together with Cantarini, provided the technique referenced in the work.<SEP>A researcher involved in research utilizing q-version summation techniques for constructing q-analogues of combinatorial identities.<SEP>Campbell is an author associated with the technique used in the simplified proof of the q-analogue.

Cantarini is a researcher who, together with Campbell, provided the technique referenced in the work.<SEP>Referenced individual associated with q-series summation techniques.<SEP>A researcher involved in research utilizing q-version summation techniques alongside Campbell for constructing q-analogues of combinatorial identities.<SEP>Cantarini is an author associated with the technique used in the simplified proof of the q-analogue.

Technique

New Q-Analogue Of Gosper's Identity
A new q-analogue of Gosper's identity that is inequivalent to Yamaguchi's q-analogue.<SEP>A derived result that is inequivalent to Yamaguchi's Q-analogue and subject to further proof.

Gosper's Identity
An identity for which a new q-analogue has been derived in the study.<SEP>A classical or q-analogue identity used in summation of certain series involving q-shifted factorials.<SEP>A classical identity whose q-analogue is analyzed using the operational simplification provided by the equation.<SEP>A summation formula for which q-analogues are constructed using this equation.<SEP>Gosper's Identity is a mathematical identity for which a new q-analogue is derived in the paper.

Shifted Factorial
A mathematical construct where the 0-th value is 1 and the n-th value is defined for positive integers.<SEP>A mathematical definition representing the product of consecutive terms defined by the Pochhammer symbol formula.

Q-Shifted Factorial
A notation defined for complex numbers a where the limit as q approaches 1 is taken.

Generalized Hypergeometric Series
Serries defined with reference to Bailey's classic text for background.<SEP>The equation represents the definition of the generalized hypergeometric series _r F_s, a fundamental special function in mathematical analysis.

Modified Abel Lemma
A lemma on summation by parts that relies on the existence of a certain limit and convergence of series.<SEP>A specific version of the Abel lemma providing operational simplification for summation techniques based on q-analogues.<SEP>The concept represented by Mathematical Equation (3), specifically concerning summation by parts.<SEP>A mathematical lemma defined in the preliminaries section applied in the application of tools.

Backward Difference Operator
An operator defined for a sequence where the value is the difference between the n-th and n-minus-1-th terms.<SEP>The operator defined as $\nabla A_n = A_n - A_{n-1}$ within the equation.

Forward Difference Operator
An operator defined for a sequence where the value is the difference between the n-th and n-plus-1-th terms.<SEP>The operator defined as $\Delta \tau_n = \tau_n - \tau_{n+1}$, acting as the negative of the standard forward difference.

Bailey's Classic Text
A classic text by Bailey that provides background information relevant to generalized hypergeometric series.

Research Contributions
Contributions by Chu et al. that have inspired the work being discussed.

Yamaguchi
Yamaguchi is a researcher whose q-analogue was simplified using the technique given by Campbell and Cantarini.<SEP>An individual associated with a Q-analogue of Gosper's identity in the literature.<SEP>Yamaguchi is the reference author whose q-analogue proof is simplified within the research study.

Chu et al.
Chu et al. are researchers whose contributions have inspired the work being discussed.

Yamaguchi's Q-Analogue
A q-analogue of Gosper's identity that is inequivalent to Yamaguchi's q-analogue.<SEP>A version of Gosper's identity that is distinct from the new analogue presented.

Chu
Research contributions from Chu et al. have inspired the work being presented.

This equation defines the generalized hypergeometric series, a cornerstone of special function theory relating to basic hypergeometric series through q-analogue relationships. It represents the classical limit form (q→1) discussed in the broader context involving Campbell-Cantarini techniques and Yamaguchi's q-analogues. The equation appears in contexts discussing summation by parts, Abel lemmas, and constructions of q-analogues throughout Sections 2.1–2.3. It connects fundamental mathematical objects including Pochhammer symbols, factorials, and convergence theory for infinite series.

This equation defines the q-shifted factorial (q-Pochhammer symbol), a fundamental construct in q-calculus and basic hypergeometric series theory. The three-part definition establishes normalization, finite products, and infinite products for the q-deformation of factorials. These expressions serve as the primary notation for basic hypergeometric series _rφ_s in Bailey's framework. Under the assumption that |q| < 1, these q-shifted factorials converge appropriately and relate to classical shifted factorials through a q→1 limit. This foundational definition enables the construction of q-analogues of classical identities (including Yamaguchi's q-analogue of Gosper's identity), facilitates the modified Abel lemma approach for summation by parts, and provides notation essential for research in combinatorics, special functions, mathematical physics, and quantum algebraic structures involving q-deformation of gamma, beta functions, theta functions, and vertex operators.

This equation defines the basic hypergeometric series of type ${}_{r}\\phi_{r-1}$, a q-analogue of the classical generalized hypergeometric series. It serves as a fundamental building block in q-series theory, combinatorics, and mathematical physics. The series incorporates q-shifted factorials and converges when the base parameter satisfies |q|<1. In the surrounding context, it represents a key construction for deriving q-analogues of classical identities, connecting research on summation by parts techniques, Abel lemmas, and the work of multiple researchers in q-calculus including Bailey, Chu, Campbell, and Cantarini. This framework enables the formulation of q-analogues of combinatorial identities including Gosper's identity.

This equation defines a generalized notation for representing ratios of q-shifted factorials in basic hypergeometric series theory. It consolidates multiple q-Pochhammer symbols into a compact form useful for q-calculus computations and analysis. The notation enables systematic representation of terms in _rφ_{s-1} basic hypergeometric series, facilitating q-analogue constructions and summation identities. Essential to q-series theory and combinatorial analysis, this definition connects classical factorial theory to q-deformations while maintaining the limit relationship to classical mathematics as q → 1.

data.entity_name: Infinite q-Shifted Factorial Product Ratio (q-Hypergeometric Series Notation) (equation)
data.content: The equation defines the standard notation for infinite q-shifted factorial products in basic hypergeometric series theory. This fundamental identity expresses a ratio of q-Pochhammer symbol products, serving as the building block for q-hypergeometric series _rphi_s-1. The notation connects classical combinatorial shifted factorials through the q→1 limit, establishing it as a genuine q-analogue. This definition is crucial for constructing q-analogues of classical identities using techniques by Campbell and Cantarini. The equation establishes the notation used throughout Sections 2.1-2.3 for general q-series manipulation, hypergeometric transformations, and applications in quantum algebra, combinatorics, and statistical mechanics contexts involving q-deformed systems.

Equation (3) is the Modified Abel Lemma, a discrete calculus identity derived from summation by parts principles adapted for infinite series in $q$-calculus. It relates weighted backward differences to forward differences and boundary limits, specifically defined with non-standard signs for the $\Delta$ operator ($\tau_n - \tau_{n+1}$). This lemma is a primary tool constructed via Campbell and Cantarini methods to derive new $q$-analogues of classical identities (such as Gosper's), serving as a foundational mechanism for proving results in basic hypergeometric series contexts where standard telescoping sums are insufficient.

data.entity_name: A Further $q$-Analogue of Gosper’s Strange Series (header)
data.content: This header introduces the section dedicated to deriving a specific extension of Gosper’s Strange Series within the context of $q$-calculus. It acts as a structural divider following the theoretical preliminaries on hypergeometric series and the modified Abel lemma. The content focuses on establishing a new, inequivalent $q$-analogue of Gosper’s identity, distinct from Yamaguchi’s existing analogue. This section likely encompasses the core proof and application of the techniques referenced in the introduction, serving as a key entry point for researchers studying summation identities and $q$-series transformations in combinatorics.

Page 2 marks the beginning of Section 2, containing the paper's main results including a simplified proof of Yamaguchi's q-analogue using Campbell-Cantarini technique with modified Abel lemma, plus a new q-analogue of Gosper's identity. This section serves as the core contribution following Section 2.1-2.3 which demonstrates broader applications in q-analogue construction methodology.
